root_text.sn47.27:1.2: Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho āyasmā sāriputto āyasmantaṁ anuruddhaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn47.27:1.4: Kittāvatā nu kho, āvuso, asekho hotī”ti?
root_text.sn47.27:1.5: “Catunnaṁ kho, āvuso, satipaṭṭhānānaṁ samattaṁ bhāvitattā asekho hoti.
root_text.sn47.27:2.2: Idhāvuso, bhikkhu kāye kāyānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;
root_text.sn47.27:2.5: dhammesu dhammānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ.
root_text.sn47.27:2.6: Imesaṁ kho, āvuso, catunnaṁ satipaṭṭhānānaṁ samattaṁ bhāvitattā asekho hotī”ti.

translation_text.sn47.27:1.3: “Reverend, they speak of the one called ‘an adept’.
translation_text.sn47.27:1.5: “Reverends, an adept is someone who has completely developed the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.
translation_text.sn47.27:2.2: It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.27:2.3: They meditate observing an aspect of feelings …
translation_text.sn47.27:2.5: principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.27:2.6: An adept is someone who has completely developed the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.”
